fact: The coal tip had been accumulating for roughly twenty years, deposited on natural springs that were not properly investigated
source: Report of the Tribunal appointed to inquire into the Disaster at Aberfan (Davies Report), 1967, HL 316 / HC 553

fact: The tip liquefied and slid at approximately 9:15 a.m. on 21 October 1966
source: National Coal Board records and the Davies Tribunal Report; corroborated by BBC and National Archives accounts

fact: The slide moved at roughly thirty miles per hour toward the village and school
source: The Davies Tribunal Report (1967) and subsequent geological analyses estimated the flow speed; widely cited as travelling at high speed. The ~30 mph figure is commonly reported.

fact: 144 people died, 116 of whom were children (mostly at Pantglas Junior School)
source: Report of the Tribunal appointed to inquire into the Disaster at Aberfan, 1967; also The Charity Commission and Welsh Government records

fact: The National Coal Board knew about the underground springs beneath the tip and placed the tip there regardless
source: Report of the Tribunal appointed to inquire into the Disaster at Aberfan (1967) — the Tribunal found the NCB guilty of 'ignorance, ineptitude, and a failure of communications' and confirmed knowledge of the springs

fact: Local council and residents had raised concerns and requested the tip be moved, but the National Coal Board refused
source: Davies Tribunal Report (1967); also documented in 'Aberfan: Government and Disaster' by Iain McLean and Martin Johnes (2000)
